title: Mod-seq: drawing DMS modification sites onto the ribosome with high-throughput sequencing

blocks.text: If you look only at the data table, the most common misreading of Mod-seq is to take every reverse-transcriptase stop counted in the sequencing as a DMS modification, and hence as a structural call. But its raw signal contains, mixed in from the start, non-DMS peaks such as endogenous rRNA methylation, and without first subtracting these background peaks the strong signal is read off-target. That reading is wrong. The essence of Mod-seq is to connect DMS chemical probing with high-throughput sequencing: DMS modifies RNA in living cells, reverse transcription stops in front of the modified site, and sequencing then reads out the positions of these reverse-transcriptase blocks at high throughput. What it actually reports is still the chemical accessibility of A and C on their Watson-Crick pairing face: the N1 of A and the N3 of C are more readily methylated when they are not paired, not occluded by a protein or ligand, and not protected by a complex conformation. In other words, what Mod-seq reports first is whether a class of chemical sites is accessible in living cells, not directly whether "this base is definitely paired" or "this base is definitely unpaired", and still less does it assign an equal structural label to every residue on the rRNA or the ribosome.

On an object as high-abundance and structurally complex as the ribosome, this boundary must be held especially firmly for FoldBridge. Because Mod-seq inherits the chemistry of DMS, it follows the same A/C target boundary: when the signal falls on A/C, it reflects the accessibility of the pairing face; G/U were never the principal interpretable objects under this DMS readout in the first place. FoldBridge can preserve the raw observations and can display experimental signal on its web pages; but as soon as anything enters default structure colouring, interpretive text or ANNOCONFIDENCE / FEC positive confidence, the raw signal must first pass checks on assay chemistry and readout context before it can become a candidate for default colouring or confidence. For Mod-seq, this check likewise begins with `DMS_AC_ONLY`, only applied within the high-abundance, structurally complex context of the rRNA and the ribosome.
From DMS modification to sequencing readout: the reverse-transcriptase block falls on A/C

blocks.legend: **Figure 1. Locating DMS modification sites with high-throughput sequencing.** Talkish et al. 2014 (PMID 24664469, DOI `10.1261/rna.042218.113`). This figure breaks down the workflow and readout of Mod-seq. The legend describes: cellular RNA treated with DMS is randomly fragmented, ligated with specific 5′ and 3′ adapters, reverse transcribed and circularised for library construction, and then PCR introduces the Illumina sequencing sequences and adds a 6-nt barcode for pooled sequencing; a genome browser (IGV) is then used to compare the number of 5′ read ends between the DMS-treated (+) and untreated (−) samples, where the peak in the untreated sample corresponds to a known endogenous rRNA methylation site, and only the difference between the two represents a true chemical modification site. The legend also points out specifically that, in the zoomed-in view of the 5.8S rRNA, the reverse-transcriptase blocks caused by DMS mostly fall on A and C, and agree with the known single-stranded regions.
body: What the reader should most remember from this figure is that Mod-seq measures not "structure" itself, but the stop signal that DMS modification leaves in the sequencing, and this stop signal is concentrated on A/C in the first place. It is in essence an in vivo DMS + sequencing readout: the chemistry is still the same A/C chemistry, only the detection method is changed to high-throughput sequencing, and background peaks such as endogenous rRNA methylation must first be subtracted before approaching the true chemical modification sites. For FoldBridge, this means that even though the reverse-transcriptase blocks agree with the known single-stranded regions, every row entering the database must still return to the assay model for interpretation: a stop on A/C can be read as an accessibility clue, but the strength of this clue is still jointly influenced by pairing, tertiary structure, protein or ligand protection, endogenous modification and sequencing background. Changing the readout to sequencing does not change this A/C boundary.
blocks.label: Mod-seq F1
blocks.legendText: Mapping DMS modification sites with high-throughput sequencing. ( A ) RNA from DMS-treated cells is randomly fragmented, ligated to specific 5′ (blue) and 3′ (green) adapter oligonucleotides, and reverse transcribed. cDNA is circularized and products containing 5′ adapter sequences are reduced via subtractive hybridization. Remaining cDNA products are PCR amplified to introduce Illumina library sequences. Each library is given a unique 6-nt barcode and pooled for high-throughput sequencing. Reads containing the 5′ adapter sequence are removed from further analysis. ( B ) Genome browser view (IGV) showing the number of 5′ read ends from DMS treated (+, purple) and untreated (−, blue) samples. The peak in the “no DMS” sample corresponds to a known site of endogenous rRNA methylation. The difference between these plots is shown in red, after shifting peaks 1 nt 5′ to represent actual sites of chemical modification. ( C ) Enlarged genome browser view of structure probing on the 5.8S rRNA. Reverse-transcriptase blocks due to DMS modification are mostly A and C nucleotides, and map to known single-stranded regions in 5.8S rRNA.

blocks.text: Base-composition evidence: the strong signal comes almost only from A and C

blocks.legend: **Figure 2. DMS modification sites identified by Mod-seq in vivo in yeast.** Talkish et al. 2014 (PMID 24664469, DOI `10.1261/rna.042218.113`). This figure directly gives the experimental evidence for the A/C boundary. The legend describes: taking the 522-nt SRP RNA (SCR1) as an example, the significantly enriched DMS modification sites are represented by coloured bars indicating the relative modification strength (fold enrichment); and when all significant modification sites are tallied by the four bases, the sites with high fold enrichment appear almost only on A and C, while even G that is significantly modified has a clearly smaller enrichment value.
body: This figure is almost an experimental footnote to `DMS_AC_ONLY`: when Talkish et al. split all the significant sites by base, the distribution of the strong signal falls almost entirely on the two base types A/C. This is precisely the core embodiment of Mod-seq inheriting the DMS boundary: the modification features it can see ultimately come from the chemical accessibility readout of A/C. So the interpretation of these sites is likewise constrained by `DMS_AC_ONLY`: a high fold enrichment on A/C suggests the pairing face is more accessible, and a low signal may suggest pairing or protection, but both judgements require context. G/U do not provide the same DMS signal under this readout, and they should not be treated as positive evidence for a structural feature simply because they also have enrichment values in the table. For FoldBridge, this figure shows why the default layer must separate interpretable A/C signal from non-target positions: the method's own data already show that the strong signal is bound to A/C.
blocks.label: Mod-seq F2
blocks.legendText: Sites of yeast in vivo DMS modification identified by Mod-seq. ( A ) Example of Mod-seq sites on the 522-nt-long SRP RNA ( SCR1 ). Nucleotides with significantly enriched sites of DMS modification are shown with colored bars depicting relative modification strength (fold enrichment). ( B ) Nucleotide composition of all significant modification sites identified. Bar graph shows the number of significant modifications at each of the four RNA nucleotide bases. Bars are colored to represent the distribution of fold enrichment for each nucleotide. Sites with high fold enrichment are almost exclusively As and Cs, while significant modification of Gs have smaller enrichment values.

blocks.text: Footprinting of protein binding sites: the A/C signal placed in the three-dimensional context of the ribosome

blocks.legend: **Figure 3. Footprinting the binding site of an RNA-binding protein with Mod-seq.** Talkish et al. 2014 (PMID 24664469, DOI `10.1261/rna.042218.113`). This figure puts the Mod-seq signal back into the three-dimensional structure of the ribosome. The legend describes: comparing the Mod-seq results of an L26 deletion mutant with wild-type cells, combined with the X-ray crystal structure model of the yeast 80S ribosome (PDB: 3U5F, 3U5G, 3U5H, 3U5I); the nucleotides whose in vivo DMS modification is enhanced in the absence of the L26 protein, coloured by fold-enrichment value, cluster precisely around the L26 binding site.
body: This figure puts the strength of Mod-seq into its most natural context, the ribosome. It shows that the change in A/C accessibility is not an isolated number: when the L26 protein is removed, the A/C sites previously occluded by the protein become more accessible, the DMS modification is enhanced accordingly, and these sites cluster together in the three-dimensional structure, outlining precisely the footprint of protein binding. This corroborates exactly the boundary set out at the start of the article: a decrease or increase in the DMS signal may come from pairing, and may come from protein or ligand protection, and the two must be distinguished by context. For FoldBridge, this is a reminder: the A/C signal of Mod-seq is especially affected by protein protection, tertiary structure and endogenous modification in the rRNA / ribosome context, so when these signals are written into the default layer, it is all the more important to emphasise that they are "A/C accessibility clues interpretable under a particular assay and a particular context", not direct pairing conclusions. G/U positions still do not enter this chain of positive evidence.
blocks.label: Mod-seq F3
blocks.legendText: Footprinting the binding site of an RNA binding protein. ( A ) Mod-seq probing results in the L26 deletion mutant and wild-type cells. ( B ) Views of the X-ray-crystal structure model of the yeast 80S ribosome generated with Pymol (PDB: 3U5F, 3U5G, 3U5H, 3U5I). The large ( left ) and small ( right ) subunits are shown in different shades of gray ( upper ). Ribosomal protein L26 is shown in a transparent surface rendering. Nucleotides with increased modification by DMS in the absence of L26 protein in vivo are shown in colors representing their fold-enrichment values. Magnified views showing residues with the largest differences in DMS sensitivity ( lower ; red and orange nucleotides). These nucleotides cluster around the site of L26 binding.

blocks.text: How FoldBridge should write about Mod-seq

Mod-seq is a DMS-based method in the literal sense (DMS treatment + high-throughput sequencing), so it directly inherits the core rule `DMS_AC_ONLY`, only applied within the high-abundance, protein- and tertiary-structure-rich context of the rRNA and the ribosome. A/C are targetable bases; G/U/T/unknown are non-target bases. Even where G/U/T/unknown rows carry numbers, they can only be retained as raw provenance, debug information or download-table fields, and should not automatically enter positive confidence simply because they were measured on the ribosome. Talkish et al.'s own base-composition analysis (Figure 2) has already shown that the strong signal belongs almost only to A/C, so this rule is not an externally imposed constraint, but a way of writing that is consistent with the data itself.

The default structure figure in ANNOJOIN should read the `visual_colormap_value` after the targetability mask, rather than reading raw values directly. Only then is the colour that readers see on the structure page a colour interpretable under the Mod-seq (i.e. in vivo DMS + sequencing) assay model. The requirements of ANNOCONFIDENCE / FEC are higher still: only A/C rows that pass the assay targetability, mapping, claim and context gates may enter the positive confidence candidate set. This chemistry gate is identical to the one in the DMS article; the only difference is that the Mod-seq signal often falls in an environment such as the ribosome that is highly modulated by proteins and endogenous modifications, so the context gate is all the more important: endogenous rRNA methylation peaks, protein protection and tertiary structure may all alter the A/C readout.

This is not a weakening of Mod-seq. On the contrary, it respects its strengths. Mod-seq is very useful for the accessibility of the A/C pairing face in living cells, especially on the ribosome, and can also outline protein binding footprints as in Figure 3; the error occurs when this strength is extended into "a uniform reactivity for every base on the ribosome". The boundary FoldBridge must hold is that "an A/C stop count was tallied on the ribosome" and "this count can serve as structural evidence" are not the same thing. The more complex the context, the more chemical reactivity, accessibility, base pairing, tertiary protection, protein/ligand protection, endogenous modification and sequencing bias must be discussed separately.

So a Mod-seq technical article should not write only "the signal is high at a certain site" or "we covered the entire rRNA". A more complete sentence should read: in the in vivo DMS + sequencing assay of Mod-seq, this A/C site has a relatively high accessibility signal; if mapping, coverage, background (including endogenous modification) and context all pass the checks, this signal can serve as support for structural interpretation. Spelling out the endogenous-modification background check is wordier, but it avoids mistaking natural methylation on the rRNA for a DMS pairing call.
There is no real FoldBridge accession here, so what follows is a clearly labelled illustrative scenario, used to show how a single Mod-seq profile separates into three different layers.

Imagine a Mod-seq profile flowing into FoldBridge: it comes from a round of in vivo DMS + sequencing experiment, covering a number of consecutive sites on a stretch of rRNA, with non-DMS peaks such as endogenous methylation also mixed into the background. In the ANNOJOIN visual track, this profile is naturally split into two classes: sites falling on A/C, and sites falling on G/U/T or at positions that are unresolved. The former are DMS-interpretable targets, the latter are not; and even the A/C rows must first have background peaks such as endogenous modification subtracted before they can approach the true chemical modification sites.

The conclusion this division gives the reader is very direct: a per-position Mod-seq observation is not equivalent to per-position DMS positive support. For A/C, DMS in the ribosome context can provide a strong accessibility clue, and can even reflect the footprint of protein binding; for G/U/T/unknown, the value is first of all raw provenance. Only the former, after passing the mapping and context gates (including the judgement of endogenous modification and protein protection), may enter ANNOCONFIDENCE / FEC positive confidence.

If every site on this profile were coloured by its raw value, the page would look fuller, but the interpretation would get worse. Non-target positions should not participate in the default DMS positive signal; rows at unresolved positions cannot pretend to have already been mapped to reliable structural coordinates; and background peaks caused by endogenous modification cannot be taken as evidence of DMS accessibility. Instead, the layer that is A/C-targetable and has had the background subtracted is the Mod-seq layer that readers should see first. The remaining rows can still be traced in the details, because they have value for provenance, debugging and reanalysis, but they should not masquerade as the same kind of evidence.

What this illustrative scenario really wants to say is not about any one number, but that the same Mod-seq profile lives as three identities at once in the system: the raw observation, the visualisation signal and the confidence candidate. These three layers each mind their own business: the raw observation layer records "what the experiment measured", the visualisation signal layer shows only "what can be read out within the interpretive boundary of in vivo DMS + sequencing, with the endogenous-modification background already subtracted", and the confidence layer receives only the interpretable evidence that has passed the gates. Once a popular-science article kneads them into one, the worst outcome is not that a few points are left undrawn, but that the reader mistakes a background peak from endogenous rRNA methylation, or a non-target signal falling on G/U/T, for evidence of A/C accessibility that DMS probed on the ribosome; and on an rRNA highly modulated by proteins and endogenous modifications, such a misreading would be propagated manyfold by the automated layer.

So the correct ending for a Mod-seq article is not "a high signal proves the base is unpaired", nor "covering the entire rRNA is equivalent to explaining the entire ribosome", but: Mod-seq can, in living cells and in the context of the rRNA and the ribosome, provide a powerful A/C accessibility clue for RNA structure interpretation, and can even outline protein binding footprints; in FoldBridge, such a clue becomes part of structural interpretation and confidence only after passing `DMS_AC_ONLY` and the subsequent gates.
